title: Governance and Mandate
domainConfidenceNote: The existence of an organisational capability gap is confirmed by Honda VP-level executives on the record in December 2024, and the specific gaps (understaffed platform/infrastructure, undersized cybersecurity) are corroborated by two independent internal sources: the Engineering Skills Map and the July 2026 project allocation. Honda's July 2026 scope communication and the project allocation together partially resolve the role question - an Execute direction is now evidenced through both product scope and Honda-directed infrastructure work - but governance structure and long-horizon funding commitment remain without a documented answer. The Honda VP admission is a strong signal but remains a single internal source and cannot be independently verified from the repository. Confidence stays Medium: the role signal is now stronger, but it was arrived at implicitly, not through an explicit negotiated agreement. The pattern of governance failure in comparable OEM-software subsidiary relationships (CARIAD, Ford-Argo) is well-documented from external sources and raises the cost of continued ambiguity.

signals.label: Acknowledged Capability Gap
signals.claim: Honda VP-level executives explicitly stated in December 2024 that DriveMode was acquired for capabilities Honda lacks and that DriveMode is tasked with training Honda employees in "modern SW development best practices." This is a direct, on-record admission that Honda does not consider itself capable of building this software internally. The admission is structurally significant: it defines DriveMode's mandate by negation - it exists because Honda cannot do this itself.
signals.sources: Honda VP-level executive statements (`raw/2025Q1_january_2025_all_hands_strategic_alternatives.md`); single internal source - strong signal but not independently verified from repository.

signals.label: Structural Mismatch
signals.claim: The Honda/DriveMode relationship has unresolved governance, informally defined decision rights, and a software cost allocation model (global/local revenue ratio) that creates conflicting incentives - documented across multiple independent repository sources from 2017 to 2026. A July 2026 working note describes a concrete operational manifestation: DriveMode-led analytics work (KPIs, self-serve dashboards) is blocked from reaching production; Honda's only analytics request is connectivity to their governance platform; strategy meetings are consumed by realigning Honda on current DriveMode reality rather than planning next steps; and the Honda counterparty (UXC Connected) is described as focused on vehicle delivery execution rather than strategic partnership. These working-note observations are Low confidence in isolation; they are consistent with the governance gap documented across the primary sources.
signals.sources: `raw/2025Q1_january_2025_all_hands_strategic_alternatives.md`; `wiki/brain-dumps/2026-06-25_software_cost_allocation_and_strategy.md`; `raw/2026Q2_organizational_capability_assessment.md`; `sources/2026-06-25_engineering_department_skills_map.md` - four independent signals on governance gap; `wiki/brain-dumps/2026-07-06_capacity_honda_alignment_pitch_reframing.md` - Low confidence working note, used as context only.

signals.label: Honda-Defined Execution Scope
signals.claim: Honda has communicated DriveMode's expected deliverables as: (1) RoadSync Duo development, operation, and improvement - monitoring, customer support, OTA updates, platform enhancements, KPI reporting, and A-SPICE compliance, against target platforms K4BA/K3NA/K3NF; (2) RoadSync Unified development - technical/feasibility reviews and prototype development across companion apps and DM servers. An internal Platform 2.0 presentation by DriveMode's Product Management Team Lead states that "expanding RoadSync Duo was always part of the long-term vision shared between Honda and Drivemode," and cites K4RA project, Gen 2, and ICE expansion initiatives as the Honda-named programmes that drove the need for a scalable software foundation - the first named documentation of Honda multi-vehicle expansion direction in the repository. This document is DriveMode-authored, not a direct Honda communication; treat directionally.
signals.sources: `raw/2026Q3_honda_drivemode_expected_scope.md`; `raw/2026Q3_platform_2_0_presentation.md` - treat as directional; Honda multi-vehicle direction is a DriveMode-authored statement, not a direct Honda communication.

signals.label: Honda-Directed Non-Discretionary Work
signals.claim: The July 2026 project allocation reveals a category of backend infrastructure work that extends beyond product scope and is explicitly Honda-aligned rather than DriveMode-discretionary: migration to Honda One ID (2 engineers, approximately 6 months), migration of all SaaS tooling to Honda-managed systems (2 engineers, approximately 3 months), Honda L1 monitoring and operations tooling (1 engineer, approximately 3 months), and a Terraform Cloud migration described as conditional on Honda's adoption decision. This pattern - Honda directing specific infrastructure choices that consume engineering capacity outside product deliverables - is a governance signal: it indicates Honda is exercising technical direction over DriveMode's platform decisions without a formal governance structure defining how those decisions are made or reviewed.
signals.sources: `raw/2026Q3_project_allocation_july2026.md`.

signals.label: Governance Failure Mode Precedent
signals.claim: VW's CARIAD programme and Ford's Argo AI investment are the two most documented OEM-software subsidiary failure modes. CARIAD: governance ambiguity between the software subsidiary and parent hardware engineering teams caused cascading delays and a EUR 14B loss before the programme was restructured. Ford-Argo: Ford invested approximately USD 1B in Argo AI and wound it down in 2022 after failing to define a clear path from research capability to vehicle production - the relationship lacked a shared definition of what the subsidiary was for. Both cases share a common failure pattern: the OEM acquired or funded a software capability without resolving governance and mandate at the outset.
signals.sources: VW Group financial disclosures (CARIAD); Ford investor communications (Argo AI wind-down, 2022); these are external references - independent verification recommended.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: The Innovator's Dilemma applied to software subsidiaries
crossIndustryPatterns.body: Large hardware-centric companies often acquire software capability in a separate entity to avoid disrupting their core operations. The recurring pattern is that the acquired entity is given enough independence to build capability but not enough mandate to make consequential decisions - leaving it effective at execution and blocked at strategy. The governance question is not usually resolved at acquisition; it surfaces as a series of operational conflicts that accumulate until a formal structure is imposed or the relationship breaks down.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: Agile vs. Waterfall governance zones
crossIndustryPatterns.body: Software development culture (iterative, fast-paced, continuous delivery) operates on fundamentally different decision cadences from traditional vehicle programme management (plan-driven, milestone-gated, multi-year horizons). Successful automotive software organisations - Stellantis STLA, Bosch Connected Mobility, BMW Group IT - have established explicit "governance zones" where different decision models apply to different parts of the stack. Without those zones, every operational disagreement becomes a governance conflict.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: The CARIAD/Ford failure template
crossIndustryPatterns.body: The pattern in both cases was: capability acquired or built, mandate left implicit, governance conflicts managed informally, until a programme delay or investment review forced a reckoning. The reckoning in both cases was more disruptive than a formal governance design at the outset would have been. The lesson is not that software subsidiaries fail - it is that the cost of deferred governance is paid eventually, usually at the worst time.

implications: DriveMode can increasingly see what near-term work is expected through RFQ-linked deliverables and headcount justification tied to RoadSync Duo, Platform 2.0, and IVI features. The July 2026 project allocation adds another layer: Honda is also directing specific infrastructure migrations that consume engineering capacity and shape platform architecture. That near-term clarity is real and workable. What remains unresolved is equally consequential: the governance structure for deciding what happens when DriveMode's technical or product judgment diverges from Honda's current direction, and the level of committed support for building platform capabilities outside the currently justifiable scope. Those questions are still being managed informally through stakeholder alignment rather than formal agreement. Honda's VP-level acknowledgement that DriveMode was acquired because Honda lacks these capabilities establishes a dependency - but a dependency is not a mandate. The Honda-directed infrastructure migrations in the project allocation show Honda exercising technical direction without a formal mechanism for DriveMode to surface concerns, propose alternatives, or escalate disagreement. That combination - acknowledged dependency plus informal direction - is the structural condition that produced governance failure in both CARIAD and Argo AI. This framework places the governance question in front of Honda leadership not as a blocker, but as a proposal. The evidence in this domain describes what a sound governance model needs to address. Honda's response to the strategy as a whole is what will shape the mandate - the governance and relationship structure is an output of that conversation, not a precondition for having it.

tensions.title: The Governance Tension: Autonomy vs. Integration
tensions.signal: Honda VP-level acknowledgement that DriveMode was acquired because Honda lacks software capability implies DriveMode needs operational independence to apply that capability - requiring autonomy from Honda's slower, plan-driven processes.
tensions.counterSignal: Honda is simultaneously directing specific infrastructure migrations (One ID, SaaS tooling, L1 monitoring) that require deep integration with Honda systems and consume a material portion of DriveMode's engineering capacity without a formal decision framework.
tensions.theTension: DriveMode is expected to move faster than Honda as a justification for its existence, while being operationally directed by Honda in ways that reduce the discretionary capacity needed to do so. Resolving this requires either a formal governance zone that separates Honda-directed work from DriveMode-discretionary investment, or an explicit renegotiation of scope.

tensions.title: The Mandate Tension: Implicit Dependency vs. Formal Agreement
tensions.signal: Honda's VP-level statement and the Honda-directed project allocation together establish that Honda depends on DriveMode for software capability it does not have internally - creating a de facto mandate.
tensions.counterSignal: No documented formal mandate exists. The role question is being answered implicitly through scope communications and project allocations, not through a written agreement that defines decision rights, escalation paths, or investment commitments.
tensions.theTension: The absence of a formal mandate is not neutral - it creates asymmetric risk. Honda can redirect DriveMode's priorities through informal scope communications; DriveMode has no documented basis for declining or negotiating. The CARIAD and Argo AI cases suggest that this asymmetry resolves badly when programme pressure increases.

requirements.description: A documented structure defining decision rights between Honda 2W and DriveMode for product roadmap, technology choices, and investment priorities - with a clear escalation path for when judgments diverge. This includes defining which infrastructure decisions Honda directs and which DriveMode owns, so that Honda-aligned work and DriveMode-discretionary investment can be tracked and funded separately.

A financial model capable of evaluating and funding platform investments with long ROI horizons, ongoing operational cost structures, and utilisation-based value measurement - distinct from project-based hardware cost models. Without this, Honda-directed non-discretionary work and DriveMode platform investment compete for the same unallocated engineering capacity with no principled basis for prioritisation.

A formal, written statement of DriveMode's role that can be referenced when Honda's direction and DriveMode's technical or product judgment conflict - not managed informally through stakeholder alignment. This statement should address at minimum: what DriveMode is accountable for deciding, what Honda retains authority over, and how disagreement is resolved.

An explicit review - by Honda and DriveMode leadership jointly - of the CARIAD and Argo AI failure patterns and how current structural conditions compare. This is not a claim that the same outcome is likely; it is a proposal that the failure template is well-documented enough to be worth examining before the conditions that triggered it are fully present.

title: The Future of Rider Ownership
domainConfidenceNote: This domain now has the strongest primary rider voice in the knowledge base. Evidence includes 35 qualitative interviews and a 301-respondent EICMA survey across Germany, France, and Italy (conducted jointly by DriveMode and Honda's Connected Technology Development Department in 2025), alongside 20+ independent sources from app stores, owner forums, and enthusiast press. The European research provides direct, methodologically rigorous willingness-to-pay data and feature priority rankings at point of purchase. Competitor cases are confirmed from public disclosures. Confidence is High for Western and European rider behaviour. The one remaining gap - rider voice from Japan and Southeast Asia, Honda's core volume markets - means that signals anchored in European data cannot be assumed to transfer directly to those markets without further research.

signals.label: Ownership vs. Access Framing
signals.claim: Riders draw a sharp distinction between features they feel they own (ride data, permanent access, safety functions) and services they access (navigation subscriptions, voice commands). The highest frustration comes from OEMs holding personal ride data hostage after subscription cancellation - ranked as a top frustration across app store reviews, owner forums, and the European qualitative research. In the EICMA survey, Digital Key was the lowest-priority feature (46% said "No Need"), consistent with rider resistance to paying for features perceived as access fees rather than owned capabilities.
signals.sources: app store reviews (Honda, Kawasaki, BMW), owner forums (ADVrider, UK-Yankee), enthusiast press (20+ independent sources); `raw/2026Q2_europe_rider_voice_research.md` (35 qualitative interviews + 301 EICMA survey, Germany/France/Italy, 2025).

signals.label: Willingness to Pay - Safety and Security Outcomes
signals.claim: Riders resist paying for pre-installed hardware but will pay for high-stakes outcomes - theft tracking and crash detection. In Europe, riders across Germany, France, and Italy are already spending €200-500+ on third-party anti-theft devices (Senti Drive, KOMOBI); dealers sell these at high volume because no OEM equivalent exists. In the 301-rider EICMA survey, Theft Alert ranked #1 in single-most-important-feature voting (28.3%), with 55% rating it Must Have and a further 41.7% Nice to Have. Globally, Calimoto Premium ($60/yr) has reached 3M+ users - demonstrating proven subscription acceptance when the outcome (scenic route finding) is unambiguously valued.
signals.sources: Monimoto (£149 + £36/yr), BikeTrac (£299 + £99/yr), EatSleepRIDE Crashlight ($49/yr); Kawasaki Canada voice subscription near-universal rejection; Calimoto App Store reviews; `raw/2026Q2_europe_rider_voice_research.md` (35 qualitative interviews + 301 EICMA survey, Germany/France/Italy, 2025).

signals.label: Vehicle Diagnostics as Core Ownership Need
signals.claim: European riders across all three countries rank Vehicle Diagnostics as the #1 feature in qualitative card sorting (26 of 35 riders), and 62% of 301 surveyed riders rate it Must Have - the second-highest Must Have score in the dataset. The core need is pre-ride visibility of tire pressure, brake fluid, oil level, coolant, battery status, and service schedule, enabling riders to maintain their vehicle safely and protect warranty and resale value. Dealers across France, Germany, and Italy confirm customers frequently cannot interpret vehicle warning lamps and must visit the dealer solely to diagnose what a light means before booking a service appointment.
signals.sources: `raw/2026Q2_europe_rider_voice_research.md` (35 qualitative interviews + 301 EICMA survey, Germany/France/Italy, 2025).

signals.label: Connected Feature Purchase Intent
signals.claim: 79% of the 301 EICMA respondents intend to use connected features in their next vehicle purchase. This includes 70% of currently non-connected riders - indicating that intent is not limited to early adopters already using connectivity. The Honda detailed customer journey model explicitly extends the customer relationship through after-purchase, service, inspection, and replacement stages, treating post-sale lifecycle as a strategic zone rather than a support function.
signals.sources: `raw/2026Q2_europe_rider_voice_research.md` (301 EICMA survey, 2025); `raw/2026Q2_honda_detailed_customer_journey_breakdown.md`.

signals.label: Competitor Failure
signals.claim: Harley-Davidson H-D Connect was discontinued August 2020 due to an insufficient user base, despite hardware already deployed in the fleet. A community petition to restore the service remained active as of 2026.
signals.sources: triketalk.com, hdforums.com, hdlivewireforum.com.

signals.label: Competitor Success - Subscription Models Beyond Western Markets
signals.claim: Indian and Chinese competitors are building operational platforms for lifecycle ownership with tiered subscriptions, measurable KPIs, and multi-model deployment. Hero MotoCorp's Battery-as-a-Service model (monthly subscription from Rs 1,242/month including battery replacement guarantee) and CFMOTO's RIDE app (geofenced anti-theft with 4G remote lock, digital key, OTA firmware updates) demonstrate that connected subscription models are operational beyond Western markets. TVS's Crash Alert system (automatic GPS alert to emergency contacts) adds a third concurrent implementation.
signals.sources: Hero COE structure (67% faster rollout; 19% MAU uplift; 30% service bookings); `sources/2026-06-26_hero_deep_dive.md`; `sources/2026-06-26_cfmoto_deep_dive.md`; `sources/2026-06-26_tvs_deep_dive.md`.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: Hardware + Service (e.g., Apple, Peloton)
crossIndustryPatterns.body: Successful ecosystems create a software layer that drives hardware upgrades and subscription revenue through a superior, integrated experience. The value the customer perceives accumulates over time in the relationship, not in the initial purchase.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: Data Portability and Trust (e.g., GDPR, Health Tech)
crossIndustryPatterns.body: Mature digital markets trend toward giving users more control over their personal data to build trust. In health and financial services, the shift from data custody to user-controlled portability has been both a regulatory response and a competitive differentiator for platforms that moved early.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: Open vs. Closed Ecosystems (e.g., Android vs. iOS)
crossIndustryPatterns.body: A foundational strategic choice exists between the faster scale of an open platform versus the greater control and potential value capture of a closed ecosystem. The outcome of this choice shapes partner economics, third-party developer incentives, and the OEM's long-term leverage over the customer relationship.

implications: The European primary research resolves a prior evidence gap and strengthens the central claim: rider ownership is a commercial and relational asset that begins at the moment of purchase and extends through service, resale, and the next vehicle decision. The fact that 79% of surveyed riders intend to use connected features in their next purchase - including a majority of currently non-connected riders - indicates that OEMs who fail to own the connected layer are not protecting the status quo; they are ceding a growing share of the rider relationship to third-party services. The battle for rider ownership is not about having a mobile app; it is about controlling the lifecycle value stream (service, accessories, financing, resale, and the next vehicle purchase). Losing this battle risks relegating the OEM to a hardware provider, while others - already including third-party anti-theft device sellers, scenic route apps, and Chinese OEM entrants offering full-map TFT navigation at lower price points - capture the ongoing relationship.

tensions.title: The Monetization Tension: Proven Niche Payments vs. Broad Subscription Resistance
tensions.signal: Riders demonstrate a clear willingness to pay for specific, high-stakes outcomes - theft tracking (55% Must Have in European survey; €200-500+ already spent on third-party devices), crash alerts, and scenic route navigation (Calimoto 3M+ users at $60/yr).
tensions.counterSignal: The same riders show near-universal rejection of broad subscription models for general access and features they feel should be included with the vehicle. Kawasaki Canada's voice command subscription was met with rejection; paywalling pre-installed hardware features is described as "sacrilege."
tensions.theTension: How to capture proven willingness to pay for specific outcomes without triggering widespread resistance to a general subscription model. The evidence suggests the distinction riders make is outcome-specificity and stakes clarity, not price level.

tensions.title: The Experience Tension: Brand Control vs. Feature Excellence
tensions.signal: A strong, controlled brand experience is a core tenet of a premium OEM strategy.
tensions.counterSignal: OEM apps are systemically rated as inferior to best-in-class third-party apps for core jobs-to-be-done. European dealer evidence is explicit: 90% of vehicles are delivered with a phone mount alongside the OEM display because riders default to Waze and Google Maps the moment OEM navigation falls below their reliability bar.
tensions.theTension: How to deliver the best-in-class user experience the market demands, even if it means relinquishing total brand control by partnering with superior applications. The research team's conclusion - that Honda needs the right foundational functions at a software quality level that matches its hardware reputation, not more features - sharpens this tension.

tensions.title: The Data Tension: Strategic Asset vs. User Trust
tensions.signal: The most advanced competitors are building platforms to leverage rider data as a strategic asset for improving their products and services. Honda's own customer journey model extends the relationship through post-sale lifecycle stages that depend on data continuity.
tensions.counterSignal: A top-ranked rider frustration - evidenced across app store reviews, owner forums, and European qualitative interviews - is personal ride data being held hostage or used opaquely. Riders describe subscription lock-in on ride data as a form of data theft.
tensions.theTension: How to leverage the strategic value of rider data without violating the user's sense of ownership and destroying the trust required to collect that data in the first place.

requirements.description: The ability to define, price, and deliver connected services whose value is measured by outcomes for the rider - bike found, crash alert sent, diagnostic status visible before the ride - rather than by features shipped. This requires understanding which outcomes riders will pay for, at what price point, and under what conditions, and building commercial models around those outcomes rather than feature bundles.

A capability to manage rider data in ways that earn active consent - giving riders control and visibility over their own data as a design principle, not as a compliance response to regulation. The evidence that data held hostage is a top rider frustration suggests governance design is a competitive differentiator, not only a legal requirement.

The ability to reach the rider at every stage of the Honda ownership relationship - consideration, purchase, active ownership, service, and replacement consideration - not only while riding. Honda's own customer journey model spans these stages; the question this domain raises is whether the connected platform currently has presence and value delivery across all of them, or primarily at the active riding stage.

A structured view of which connected services riders are currently purchasing from third parties (anti-theft devices, scenic navigation subscriptions, crash detection apps) and a deliberate decision about whether Honda should serve each gap directly, partner to serve it, or cede it. Dealers are already filling these gaps commercially; the absence of an OEM response is itself a strategic position, whether intentional or not.

title: Software as Competitive Infrastructure
domainConfidenceNote: This domain now draws on three distinct evidence layers: verified production metrics from motorcycle OEM competitors (Hero MotoCorp AWS case study; Ather Energy IPO and partner disclosures), documented infrastructure failure cases from both the motorcycle sector (Harley-Davidson H-D Connect) and the automotive sector (VW CARIAD, Ford Model e), and internal org composition data from two independent internal sources (Engineering Skills Map and July 2026 project allocation). Confidence is High because the competitor infrastructure benchmarks are sourced from AWS case studies and IPO-grade disclosures, not analyst projections, and the failure cases are documented in investor-grade filings and verifiable press records. The one remaining qualification is that the internal org data reflects structural composition (headcount and allocation) rather than measured delivery capability; domain expertise and execution quality are not directly observable from these sources.

signals.label: Competitor Production Benchmark
signals.claim: Hero MotoCorp's production connected vehicle platform (AWS IoT + EKS, Excelfore eSync OTA) handles 2.2 million API requests per day at 99.6% availability across 40,000 connected vehicles. The platform delivers feature rollout in one month versus a previous three months (67% faster). A Netcore Cloud engagement program on top of the platform produced a 19% uplift in monthly active users. Ather Energy operates a monthly OTA update cadence delivering 64 connected features in production as of July 2024, against an industry norm of approximately two OTA releases per year, enabled by its Google Cloud infrastructure (IoT Core, BigQuery, Pub/Sub). CFMOTO operates a dual-cloud architecture using Alibaba Cloud for domestic and Asian markets and AWS/Azure for GDPR-compliant Western regions - the first documented infrastructure-level regional differentiation in the competitor set.
signals.sources: `sources/2026-06-26_hero_deep_dive.md`; aws.amazon.com/solutions/case-studies/hero-motocorp-case-study/; netcorecloud.com/success-story/hero-motocorp-digital-growth-success-netcore-cloud/; Ather Energy IPO disclosures (NSE/BSE, May 2025); `sources/2026-06-26_cfmoto_deep_dive.md`.

signals.label: Premium Performance Connectivity Benchmark
signals.claim: ZX Moto (founded 2024) deployed a full ARAS suite on its debut 820RR using a 6-axis Bosch IMU plus front/rear radar: cornering ABS, cornering traction control, forward collision warning, blind spot detection with mirror-integrated alerts, and lane keeping assist. The companion app delivers OTA tuning of 5 riding modes (throttle response, traction control, wheelie control) and records track telemetry (lap times, G-forces, lean angles) from the onboard IMU. Anti-theft geofencing and remote ignition lockout are also in the app. Navigation is handled via phone mirroring - no proprietary navigation build. This feature set was achieved with a $13.1M Series A by outsourcing safety-critical components to Bosch, Brembo, and Öhlins while maintaining in-house ownership of engine/chassis design and software UI/UX. The result is a benchmark for what connected performance infrastructure looks like when a new entrant uses Tier 1 partners to accelerate deployment - without a proprietary cloud backend or digital twin platform.
signals.sources: `raw/2026Q3_zxmoto_china_equity_research.md` (Medium confidence - single equity research source; brand is 2 years old; all connectivity claims are design-intent, not field-validated).

signals.label: Systemic Failure Pattern
signals.claim: OEM connectivity failures are documented across multiple platforms. Harley-Davidson discontinued H-D Connect, a documented case of a proprietary connected services platform that was retired rather than sustained - representing a motorcycle-sector infrastructure failure case. Yamaha Y-Connect and Kawasaki Rideology have independent app store records of Bluetooth reliability failures. Honda RoadSync is described by named dealers across France and Italy as "absolutely not user friendly," offering only turn-by-turn navigation with no full map or moving arrow, leading riders to abandon the system and use phone mounts with Waze and Google Maps instead. Dealers report 90% of vehicles are delivered with a third-party phone mount alongside or instead of the OEM display. Internal production data from RoadSync Duo in India adds a first-party evidence point: as of August 2025, Honda's own connected vehicles in India are offline for 99.71% of ignition-on time, with only 15.1% of ride sessions having the companion app connected. Bluetooth PAN connectivity is described as "virtually non-existent" in the internal analytics report. This is the same infrastructure reliability failure documented externally for competitor platforms, now confirmed from within Honda's own production fleet.
signals.sources: H-D Connect discontinuation - referenced in KB as documented infrastructure failure case (single reference; no primary filing confirmed); app store reviews (Yamaha, Kawasaki); `raw/2026Q2_europe_rider_voice_research.md` - named dealer interviews in France, Italy, Germany (2025); `sources/2025-10-28_honda_roadsync_duo_ux_analytics_india.md` - internal Honda/DriveMode analytics, India, Aug 2025, 27 vehicles (Medium confidence: small base, potential dataset contamination from internal-use vehicles).

signals.label: Financial Cautionary Tale
signals.claim: Attempts to build proprietary, large-scale software stacks at automotive OEMs have produced catastrophic financial losses. VW CARIAD accumulated a total investment of approximately €14 billion with an operating loss of €2.1 billion in 2022 alone before VW licensed Rivian's software architecture for $5.8 billion, effectively replacing the internal build. Ford's FNV4/Model e software platform resulted in a total write-down of $19.5 billion (disclosed December 2025), with $4.7 billion in EV and software losses in 2023 and approximately $5 billion in 2024.
signals.sources: VW Group financial disclosures; InsideEVs; GermanAutopreneur; TheStack.Technology; Ford 8-K; CNBC; CBT News; Rivian 8-K.

signals.label: Internal Structural Composition
signals.claim: The engineering department consists of 51 personnel across six functional sub-teams. Platform and Infrastructure is staffed by 3 people (approximately 4% of engineering headcount) and Systems Engineering by 3 people (approximately 4% of engineering headcount) - these are the two teams that underpin scalable connected vehicle infrastructure. As of the July 2026 allocation, the Backend team's two most senior engineers are fully committed to the AWS Migration.
signals.sources: `sources/2026-06-25_engineering_department_skills_map.md`; `raw/2026Q3_project_allocation_july2026.md`; `raw/2026Q3_person_allocation_anonymized.md`.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: The Cloud Computing Shift (IaaS/PaaS)
crossIndustryPatterns.body: Companies increasingly outsource undifferentiated infrastructure (servers, databases, message queuing) to hyperscale providers (AWS, GCP, Azure) to concentrate engineering resources on their unique customer-facing applications. Hero and Ather's adoption of AWS and Google Cloud respectively follows this pattern - neither company is competing on cloud infrastructure; both are competing on what runs on top of it.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: The Rise of SaaS Platforms
crossIndustryPatterns.body: The success of platforms like Salesforce demonstrates a winning strategy of paying for a best-in-class service for a non-core function rather than building an inferior version in-house. In the motorcycle sector, Excelfore eSync for OTA and Sibros for telematics represent analogous specialist platforms that OEMs can buy rather than build.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: The Telco Capital Trap
crossIndustryPatterns.body: The telecommunications industry shows that owning and operating scaled, reliable infrastructure is a high-capital, low-margin business requiring a fundamentally different operating model than product development. The VW CARIAD and Ford Model e write-downs follow a version of this trap at automotive scale - the cost of building infrastructure from scratch overwhelmed the revenue opportunity.

implications: Software infrastructure is the foundational, non-negotiable layer for any connected vehicle strategy. An unreliable or unscalable infrastructure guarantees the failure of any rider-facing application, as the dealer testimony on Honda RoadSync and the H-D Connect discontinuation illustrate. The Hero MotoCorp production benchmark is particularly significant: a motorcycle OEM operating 2.2 million API requests per day at 99.6% availability, delivering OTA in one month instead of three, has established a production-grade infrastructure standard within the same vehicle category and market context as Honda's 2W business. This is no longer a cross-industry analogy from automotive; it is a direct in-category reference point. At the same time, the 3 FTE allocated to Platform and Infrastructure and 3 FTE to Systems Engineering, combined with the current commitment of senior engineering capacity to the AWS Migration and Gen1 backlog, represent a current allocation pattern that does not have parallel capacity for production-scale infrastructure development alongside existing commitments - a sequencing question, not a permanent state. The financial precedents from CARIAD and Ford reinforce the case against a large proprietary build, but the Hero and Ather benchmarks show that competitive infrastructure is achievable through cloud-native architecture and specialist partner platforms rather than ground-up proprietary construction.

tensions.title: The Investment Tension: The High Cost of Building vs. The High Cost of Failing
tensions.signal: The financial evidence from VW CARIAD (€14B total) and Ford Model e ($19.5B write-down) shows that large-scale proprietary software builds at automotive OEMs are exceptionally high-risk and can destroy capital at a scale that dwarfs any plausible motorcycle-segment revenue opportunity.
tensions.counterSignal: The rider voice and competitor failure evidence shows that under-investment in infrastructure reliability also destroys value: Honda RoadSync abandonment by dealers, H-D Connect discontinuation, and Yamaha/Kawasaki Bluetooth failures each represent brand equity costs with no capital write-down event to signal them.
tensions.theTension: How to make the necessary investment to achieve infrastructure reliability - as Hero has done at 99.6% availability - without replicating the proprietary build structures that failed at CARIAD and Ford. The Hero/Ather pattern (cloud-native, specialist partners, no proprietary infrastructure layer) may represent the resolution to this tension, but applying it requires governance decisions about what DriveMode builds versus buys.

tensions.title: The Control Tension: Strategic Differentiation vs. Commodity Outsourcing
tensions.signal: Platform and Infrastructure is staffed by 3 people and Systems Engineering by 3 people, with the most senior Backend engineers currently committed to the AWS Migration. This is the team composition available to sustain and evolve connected vehicle infrastructure at the current moment.
tensions.counterSignal: The most effective competitors (Hero, Ather, CFMOTO) are building on top of commodity cloud platforms and specialist OTA providers rather than building infrastructure in-house. Their engineering investment is concentrated in product and feature layers, not the infrastructure layer.
tensions.theTension: Which parts of the software stack are a true source of competitive differentiation that require in-house ownership, and which parts are commodity infrastructure where a partner platform would deliver this layer faster and at higher reliability than building it in-house? This question is not answerable from structural data alone - it requires a capability and product strategy conversation about where DriveMode's differentiated value actually lives.

requirements.description: The ability to deliver software updates to vehicles reliably, securely, and at the compliance certification level Honda requires to authorize vehicle connection - across multiple models and markets. The Ather benchmark of monthly OTA cadence across 64 features, and Hero's one-month rollout cycle versus the previous three months, define what production-grade OTA looks like in this vehicle category. The question for this domain is whether OTA is built in-house, delivered through a specialist partner platform (such as Excelfore eSync or Sibros), or some combination, and what governance ensures the compliance and certification requirements are met at each release.

The operational capability to maintain availability, performance, and security of connected vehicle services at a quality level that does not damage brand trust when connectivity is impaired. Hero's 99.6% availability at 2.2 million API requests per day is the in-category production reference. The H-D Connect discontinuation and Honda RoadSync dealer abandonment illustrate what happens when this capability is absent or insufficient. This requirement asks whether the current infrastructure team composition and architecture are sized and structured to sustain reliability at the scale Honda's connected fleet would require.

A systematic security engineering capability - not a secondary allocation within another role - covering vehicle-side, cloud-side, and data-in-transit security at the level required by a connected vehicle platform operating across multiple regulatory environments. This requirement asks whether cybersecurity is currently a named function with dedicated ownership, or whether it is distributed across other roles in a way that may create gaps as the platform scales.

A deliberate decision about how infrastructure capacity is structured: whether the 3 FTE Platform and Infrastructure team and 3 FTE Systems Engineering team are the intended long-term staffing for this function, whether that team is supplemented by specialist partner platforms, or whether the infrastructure layer is restructured around a different model. The financial failure cases suggest the risk of over-building in-house; the competitor benchmarks suggest the risk of under-investing in operational reliability. This requirement asks for an explicit position on how infrastructure capacity will be sustained and scaled.

title: Rider Lifecycle Infrastructure vs. Rider-Facing App
domainConfidenceNote: Rider preference for best-in-class third-party apps over OEM apps is strongly evidenced from Western markets (20+ sources; Calimoto 3M+ users, EatSleepRIDE with demonstrated willingness to pay for crash detection). Competitor platform strategies for Hero, TVS, Ather, and Ola Electric are confirmed from public disclosures and business press. The contrast between Hero's measurable platform outcomes and Kawasaki and Yamaha's feature-list approach is supported by independent competitor assessments. An internal Platform 2.0 presentation provides direct evidence of DriveMode's current platform scope and its explicit boundaries - specifically that identity, security, and pre/post-sale lifecycle are not included in the current platform definition, and that Platform 2.x Expansion does not appear in the monthly engineering timeline until January-February of the fiscal year. Confidence remains Medium because the financial evidence comes from automotive OEMs at significantly larger scale, the successful platform cases are India-centric, and the internal capability data is a single self-assessed source.

signals.label: Voice of Rider
signals.claim: Third-party apps that excel at a single job-to-be-done - Calimoto for scenic routing (3M+ users, $60/year Premium subscription), EatSleepRIDE for crash detection ($49/year Crashlight), REVER for route discovery - are consistently preferred over comprehensive-but-mediocre OEM apps. European primary research confirms this pattern at point of sale: dealers report 90% of vehicles are delivered with a third-party phone mount, and riders default to Waze and Google Maps because OEM navigation does not meet their quality bar. Demonstrated willingness to pay for these third-party services is high; OEM app equivalents are absent or rated unreliable.
signals.sources: app store reviews, Calimoto user base data, EatSleepRIDE pricing, enthusiast press; 20+ independent sources; `raw/2026Q2_europe_rider_voice_research.md` - dealer interviews Germany/France/Italy (2025).

signals.label: Platform Outcomes vs. Feature Lists
signals.claim: Hero MotoCorp's platform-first strategy has produced documented, measurable outcomes: 67% faster feature rollout (from 3 months to 1 month), 99.6% platform availability across 40,000 connected vehicles, 19% uplift in monthly active users, and 30% growth in service bookings. The VIDA connected EV brand achieved 117% year-on-year sales growth in October 2025. By contrast, Kawasaki's Rideology app is fragmented across four separate apps and receives user complaints about Bluetooth compatibility; the company's Connected Promotion Department has not disclosed adoption figures. Yamaha's Y-Connect app is described by users as "nearly useless" for connectivity, with Bluetooth reconnection failures and server errors across the 2025 lineup. The contrast between Hero's infrastructure investment with named technology partners (AWS, Excelfore for OTA, Netcore for CRM) and Kawasaki and Yamaha's feature-list approaches is consistent across independent competitive assessments.
signals.sources: `wiki/meta/extractions_2026Q2_competitor_strategy_depth.md`; aws.amazon.com/solutions/case-studies/hero-motocorp-case-study/; netcorecloud.com/success-story/hero-motocorp-digital-growth-success-netcore-cloud/; here.com Kawasaki press release; Google Play user reviews.

signals.label: Full-Stack Cautionary Case
signals.claim: Ola Electric pursued the most ambitious full-stack proprietary strategy of any two-wheeler OEM globally: a proprietary operating system (MoveOS), proprietary AI (Krutrim), proprietary mapping (Ola Maps), and proprietary OTA infrastructure. Despite genuine technical innovation - MoveOS 6 launched August 2025 with conversational AI and predictive service - the company experienced a market share collapse from 35%+ to approximately 15% (2025), concurrent with three rounds of layoffs totalling 1,620+ employees in 24 months and a Q1 FY26 revenue decline of approximately 50% year-on-year. The full-stack investment did not prevent commercial deterioration.
signals.sources: techstory.in, e-vehicleinfo.com, peoplematters.in; `wiki/meta/extractions_2026Q2_competitor_strategy_depth.md`.

signals.label: Internal Allocation
signals.claim: Platform 2.0 is an active IVI architecture investment explicitly designed for multi-vehicle scalability: it introduces an abstraction layer separating hardware from feature layers, targets approximately 65% deployment cost reduction per new vehicle model (from approximately 18 months / 324M yen to approximately 6 months / 108M yen), and aims to resolve 189 documented Gen 1 issues. However, DriveMode's own Product Management Team Lead explicitly scopes Platform 2.0 as referring exclusively to the embedded IVI software and its scalability capacity - it does not include identity, security, pre-sale, or post-sale capabilities. The forward-looking questions posed in the same presentation - "should we keep investing in preparation for future vehicles' digital value, and continue to aim for software monetization?" - are framed as open decisions, not resolved direction. In the July 2026 fiscal year plan, Platform 2.x Expansion does not appear in the monthly engineering timeline until January-February, indicating the broader platform expansion is not resourced within the current planning cycle. This allocation pattern is consistent with a current weighting toward product depth over lifecycle infrastructure expansion.
signals.sources: `raw/2026Q3_platform_2_0_presentation.md` (internal single source; cost figures are internal estimates, methodology not disclosed); `sources/2026-06-25_engineering_department_skills_map.md`; `raw/2026Q3_project_allocation_july2026.md`.

signals.label: Integrator Pattern
signals.claim: The most advanced competitors have adopted clear platform strategies enabling multi-model deployment, tiered subscriptions, and data-driven product development. CFMOTO's systems integrator model is the most concrete documented implementation of platform-over-product thinking in the sector: they build only the app layer and vehicle integration in-house, sourcing every platform component (cloud, safety electronics, battery, AI) from specialized partners, then own the integration layer above it. Hero MotoCorp, TVS Motor, and Ather Energy follow similar integrator patterns with 7+ model deployment each. ZX Moto provides a third archetype - a hybrid model: core IP (engine, chassis, software UI/UX) strictly in-house, while safety-critical and performance-credibility components (Bosch IMU/ECU, Brembo brakes, Öhlins suspension) are fully externalized. Distinct from the full systems integrator (CFMOTO) and from a full in-house build, this selective in-house plus Tier 1 partner model achieved superbike-level connected features with $13.1M in Series A funding.
signals.sources: `sources/2026-06-26_cfmoto_deep_dive.md`; Hero COE structure; TVS Motor Databricks + CDAO role; Ather 64-feature OTA cadence; TVS 8-year connected services track record; `raw/2026Q3_zxmoto_china_equity_research.md`.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: Operating Systems (e.g., Windows, Android)
crossIndustryPatterns.body: The most successful platforms succeed by enabling a vast ecosystem of third-party applications, creating value far beyond what the platform owner could build alone. Their primary product is the set of APIs and tools for developers.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: Content Platforms (e.g., YouTube, Netflix)
crossIndustryPatterns.body: These platforms succeed through a dual strategy: providing a world-class consumption experience while also investing heavily in original content to differentiate and lock in users.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: E-commerce (e.g., Amazon Marketplace vs. Brand.com)
crossIndustryPatterns.body: Brands constantly face the choice between leveraging a massive existing platform for its scale and traffic, versus building a proprietary product experience for greater control and brand identity.

implications: The choice between investing in Rider Lifecycle Infrastructure versus the Rider-Facing App is a fundamental choice about where defensible value is created. A Rider-Facing App strategy competes directly with best-in-class third-party apps on experience quality - and the evidence shows third-party apps are already winning on the most important rider jobs (navigation, route discovery, crash detection). Riders demonstrate clear willingness to pay for these third-party alternatives, which compounds the competitive disadvantage for any OEM app that does not match their quality bar. A Rider Lifecycle Infrastructure strategy owns the layer that third parties cannot replicate: the vehicle data stream, the rider identity, and the full customer journey data from consideration through replacement. The Ola Electric case adds a specific caution: full-stack proprietary investment in the app and infrastructure simultaneously - without the platform governance to support it - did not prevent market share collapse and financial deterioration. The Hero case offers the counter-evidence: a structured infrastructure investment with named technology partners produced measurable operational and commercial outcomes. The trade-off is that infrastructure ownership requires Honda's formal mandate to expose vehicle data, sustained long-horizon investment, and governance clarity before the value becomes visible. Attempting to compete on both without a clear primary position typically produces a rider app that is not best-in-class and an infrastructure that is never complete.

tensions.title: The Value Capture Tension: Direct Control vs. Ecosystem Scale
tensions.signal: A proprietary, all-in-one rider app gives DriveMode total control over the user experience and captures the full value of the rider relationship directly.
tensions.counterSignal: The most valuable infrastructure layers in technology history (operating systems, cloud platforms, payment rails) created greater value by enabling an ecosystem of others to build on top of them, capturing a smaller fraction of a much larger total.
tensions.theTension: How to reconcile the desire to own the entire rider experience directly (Rider-Facing App) with the evidence that the greatest defensible value often comes from owning the infrastructure layer that others cannot bypass (Rider Lifecycle Infrastructure).

tensions.title: The Resource Allocation Tension: Feature Velocity vs. Foundational Stability
tensions.signal: Business stakeholders and riders demand new, visible features - navigation improvements, vehicle status, connectivity - which pulls engineering resources toward the rider-facing application layer.
tensions.counterSignal: The evidence shows the most common failure mode for OEM apps is infrastructure instability (Bluetooth drops, connectivity failures, data gaps) - problems that are solved by deep, non-visible investment in OTA, telemetry, and identity infrastructure, not by adding features. Hero's investment in named infrastructure partners (AWS IoT, Excelfore OTA) preceded its measurable outcome improvements.
tensions.theTension: How to allocate limited engineering resources between building new, visible rider features (Rider-Facing App) and the deep, often invisible work of building the data pipeline, OTA delivery, and identity layer that a Rider Lifecycle Infrastructure strategy requires - particularly when Platform 2.x Expansion is not resourced until late in the current fiscal year.

tensions.title: The Full-Stack Risk Tension: Ambition vs. Execution Capacity
tensions.signal: The Ola Electric case shows that the most technically ambitious full-stack strategy in the two-wheeler sector - proprietary OS, AI, maps, and OTA - did not prevent a market share collapse from 35% to 15% and severe financial deterioration.
tensions.counterSignal: Hero MotoCorp's more selective infrastructure investment, using specialist partners for cloud, OTA, and CRM while building integration and platform governance in-house, produced documented commercial outcomes without the same financial fragility.
tensions.theTension: Whether the risk of full-stack ambition outweighs the control benefits, and whether a partner-led infrastructure model - as practiced by Hero, TVS, and CFMOTO - represents a more durable path - not as a constraint response but as a deliberate architecture decision that the evidence from Hero, TVS, and CFMOTO supports on its own merits.

requirements.requirement: Infrastructure Product Management (if Rider Lifecycle Infrastructure)
requirements.description: The ability to define OTA delivery, data pipelines, and identity APIs as products - with version governance, access controls, and SLAs for internal teams and external partners, not as rider-facing features.

requirements.requirement: Multi-Model Deployment Architecture (if Rider Lifecycle Infrastructure)
requirements.description: The ability to scale connected vehicle infrastructure across Honda's full model range without rebuilding per vehicle or per market.
The ability to match or exceed the reliability and experience quality of best-in-class third-party apps in the specific jobs riders prioritize - navigation, theft protection, and vehicle diagnostics.

Regardless of primary direction, the evidence from Hero, TVS, and CFMOTO suggests that managing specialist technology partners (cloud infrastructure, OTA delivery, CRM, navigation) as an integrated platform - rather than as isolated vendor relationships - is a distinct capability. This requires defined integration standards, partner SLAs, and an internal layer responsible for platform coherence above the partner stack.

title: Regional Differentiation vs. Global Scale
domainConfidenceNote: Domain signal confidence: Mixed - High for Europe, Low for Asia-Pacific. European rider voice is now well-evidenced: 35 qualitative interviews and 301 EICMA survey respondents across Germany, France, and Italy (2025) provide primary research with high confidence. Five dedicated regional analyses now exist in this KB covering India, Europe, Japan, Vietnam/SEA, and Malaysia. India now has limited primary rider data: an internal Honda/DriveMode UX analytics report (October 2025) covers 27 India Activa-E connected vehicles through August 2025, plus 10 qualitative interviews in Bangalore (only 2 of whom own the connected variant). This is the first direct India rider evidence in the KB but is not statistically projectable - it is an EV urban commuter cohort in a single city, not representative of India's broader 2W market. Japan and Southeast Asia - Honda's other primary volume markets - still have no direct rider voice. Japan is Honda's headquarters and strategic decision-making centre but ranks #8 globally by growth opportunity, and Japanese rider preferences are entirely undocumented here. The confidence level for Asia-Pacific rider needs remains Low overall. Strategic decisions about regional differentiation for Honda's core Asian markets should not be made from this evidence base alone until broader Asia-Pacific primary rider research has been conducted.

signals.label: Honda's Localization Actions
signals.claim: RoadSync Duo carries India-only security features and battery-swapping integration in specific SEA markets. Honda is a member of the Swappable Batteries Motorcycle Consortium (SBMC) alongside CFMOTO, KTM, Yamaha, and Piaggio, working to standardize light EV battery specifications across brands.
signals.sources: `sources/2026-06-23_honda_roadsync_duo_app_store.pdf`; `raw/2026Q2_organizational_capability_assessment.md`; `sources/2026-06-26_cfmoto_deep_dive.md`.

signals.label: European Rider Voice
signals.claim: Primary research across Germany (11 riders), France (12), and Italy (12) plus a 301-rider EICMA survey identifies four core European value propositions in priority order: (1) Theft Alert and Live Tracking (Theft Alert: 28.3% single most important; 55% Must Have); (2) Reliable Navigation with real-time hazard data - police, speed cameras, accidents (63% Must Have); (3) Vehicle Diagnostics (62% Must Have; #1 in qualitative card sorting); (4) Distraction-free connectivity - not using phone while riding (58.3% Must Have). 79% of surveyed riders plan to buy connected features in their next vehicle.
signals.sources: `raw/2026Q2_europe_rider_voice_research.md` (DriveMode + Honda primary research, Germany/France/Italy, 2025; 35 qualitative interviews + 301 EICMA survey).

signals.label: India Growth Market / Emergent Rider Voice
signals.claim: India ranks #1 globally for growth opportunity and connected vehicle investment in the macroeconomic dashboard (GDP growth forecast 6.8% for 2026; connected services opportunity rated High) and is the world's largest two-wheeler market by volume. The KB now contains the first direct India rider evidence: an internal Honda/DriveMode UX analytics report (October 2025) covering 27 India Activa-E connected vehicles through August 2025 and 10 qualitative interviews in Bangalore (2 connected-variant owners, 8 QC1 non-connected users). Key India-specific findings: (1) In-vehicle navigation was the primary purchase driver for the connected variant - stated explicitly in qualitative interviews; (2) Indian riders rely on the full-map view for context, not turn-by-turn alone; 82.2% of navigation trips go to general destinations, 17.2% to e:swap stations; (3) Riders prefer battery percentage over estimated range due to range estimate inaccuracy ("I cannot trust this range"); (4) Music is not used during commuting - 87 stationary vs 4 at-speed music clicks - riders lack connected headsets and do not perceive a need; (5) Call usage is near-zero: 30 total CallStart events, all stationary; (6) Settings accessed exclusively at rest; "no compromise on safety" is the stated rider position. These findings diverge materially from the European pattern, where theft alert ranks #1 and navigation is #2. Confidence remains Low: the sample is 27 vehicles in a single city, EV-only segment, with only 2 connected-variant owners in qualitative research. India-based OEMs (Hero MotoCorp CVP on AWS + Excelfore eSync OTA; Ather on Google Cloud) set the most advanced connected vehicle competitive baseline in the world.
signals.sources: `sources/2025-10-28_honda_roadsync_duo_ux_analytics_india.md` (internal Honda/DriveMode; 27 India vehicles, 10 interviews, Aug 2025; Medium confidence - small base, EV urban commuter cohort only, potential dataset contamination); `wiki/outputs/2026-07-07_regional_analysis_india.md`; `sources/2026-06-26_macroeconomic_dashboard_update.md`; `sources/2026-06-26_hero_deep_dive.md`; `sources/2026-06-26_tvs_deep_dive.md`.

signals.label: Japan HQ Paradox
signals.claim: Japan is Honda's corporate headquarters and the source of strategic direction for the global Honda 2W programme, but ranks #8 globally by growth opportunity. A weak Yen is crushing domestic demand for premium goods and making imported telematics hardware more expensive, weakening the business case for connected features on domestic models. Japanese OEMs Yamaha and Kawasaki have consistently chosen to partner for navigation (Garmin, HERE respectively) rather than build in-house - suggesting Japan-based OEM strategy is oriented toward integration rather than proprietary platform investment. Japanese rider attitudes toward connected features, data privacy, and subscription services are not documented anywhere in this KB.
signals.sources: `wiki/outputs/2026-07-07_regional_analysis_japan.md`; `sources/2026-06-26_macroeconomic_dashboard_update.md`; `raw/2026Q2_competitor_strategy_depth.md`.

signals.label: Vietnam/SEA Pre-Adoption Phase
signals.claim: Asia-Pacific represents 41.7% of the global connected motorcycle market with a 35.8% CAGR - the largest and fastest-growing segment globally. Yet the region is in a pre-adoption phase for connected services: in Malaysia, no domestic competitor (including MForce, the dominant local manufacturer) has deployed a proprietary app despite a broad model range, suggesting the market has not demanded one strongly enough to justify investment. Vietnam is ranked #2 globally by the macroeconomic dashboard (GDP 6.2% growth), but rider voice from the entire SEA region is absent. Honda RoadSync Duo carries battery-swapping integration for specific SEA markets - a product decision, not a rider-confirmed preference.
signals.sources: `wiki/outputs/2026-07-07_regional_analysis_vietnam_sea.md`; `wiki/outputs/2026-07-07_regional_analysis_malaysia.md`; ABI Research connected motorcycle market data cited in `wiki/outputs/2026-06-26_signal_strengthening_findings.md`; `sources/2026-06-26_deep_dive_mforce.md`.

signals.label: Competitor Regional Focus vs. Global Ambition
signals.claim: The most digitally advanced competitors (Hero, Ather, TVS) are almost entirely India-centric, succeeding through deep regional focus rather than global platforms. Suzuki's connected service (3.1M India downloads) is also India-only. In Europe, Chinese brands (CFMoto 800MT, Zontes 368G, Coge 900 DSX) have grown significantly in the low-mid price range (EUR 5,000-10,000) by offering full-map TFT navigation and dashcams at price points where Honda currently does not offer full-map navigation (Honda full-map starts at Africa Twin, EUR 14,000+). Italian dealers confirm this competitive shift directly. In Brazil, Shineray achieved 69.6% growth in 2025 (130,600 units) and a 7.24% peak monthly market share in 2026 - becoming the third-largest motorcycle manufacturer by targeting the gig economy delivery segment (iFood, Rappi riders) with ultra-affordable utility models, without any proprietary connected platform. ZX Moto (China, founded 2024) is the counter-case to the regional-focus-first pattern: an export-first Chinese premium performance brand that targets high-value Western markets from launch, beginning EU shipments to Poland in June 2026 with a full European 820RR rollout planned for early 2027, supported by an Italian parts hub for 3-day delivery - with no meaningful domestic Chinese market share yet. Its "Win on Sunday, Sell on Monday" WorldSSP racing strategy converted Western distributors into partners through on-track performance credibility before the product was in volume production.
signals.sources: Hero/Ather/TVS public disclosures; Suzuki Ride Connect India press release; `raw/2026Q2_europe_rider_voice_research.md` (HRE sales data + dealer interviews, 2025); 40+ competitor sources; `raw/2026Q3_shineray_brazil_equity_research.md` (Medium confidence - single equity research source, unverified primary data); `raw/2026Q3_zxmoto_china_equity_research.md` (Medium confidence - single equity research source; brand is 2 years old).

crossIndustryPatterns.title: "Glocalization" (e.g., McDonald's, Netflix)
crossIndustryPatterns.body: Successful global brands often operate on a "glocal" model: they maintain a consistent global brand and core product/platform, but allow significant regional autonomy to adapt menus, content libraries, and marketing to local tastes. Honda's existing localization actions (SBMC membership, battery-swap integration, India-only security features) suggest some version of this pattern is already in operation at the product level.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: Digital Services (e.g., Uber, Spotify)
crossIndustryPatterns.body: Digital platforms often face significant hurdles in new regions due to local payment systems, regulatory environments, mapping data availability, and consumer habits, forcing them to run quasi-independent regional operations. VinFast's use of Vietmap Live Pro for Vietnam-specific navigation (rather than Google Maps alone) is an early signal that this dynamic applies in SEA motorcycle markets.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: Automotive OEM Software Fragmentation
crossIndustryPatterns.body: In four-wheel automotive, OEMs that built separate regional software stacks for China, Europe, and North America have documented significant integration and maintenance cost burdens as the stacks diverge over time. The risk of a similar pattern in two-wheel connected services - driven by regulatory divergence (GDPR in Europe, DPDP Act in India, PDPA in Malaysia, data localisation in Vietnam) - is a structural concern that architecture decisions must address proactively.

implications: A single global software platform serving Honda's full regional footprint - India (#1 growth), Vietnam/SEA (41.7% of connected motorcycle market, 35.8% CAGR), Europe (primary rider voice, high connected intent), and Japan (HQ, #8 growth, Low domestic demand) - faces structurally divergent requirements. The European evidence base now allows confident specification of European feature priorities. India and SEA together represent the dominant growth and volume opportunity, but the absence of rider voice from both regions means the European findings cannot be extended there by assumption. The Japan HQ paradox - where strategic decisions are made in a market ranked #8 for connected vehicle opportunity - is a structural alignment risk: decisions made from a domestic Japanese market context may not serve India, Vietnam, or Indonesia. The strategic question is not whether to localise, but how to architect a platform that enables deep regional differentiation without fracturing into multiple unsustainable one-off codebases - and whether the organisational governance model supports the regional intelligence inputs needed to make those differentiation decisions well.

tensions.title: The Evidence Tension: European Confidence vs. Asia-Pacific Gap
tensions.signal: Europe now has the strongest primary rider evidence in the KB (35 qualitative interviews + 301 EICMA survey), providing actionable, high-confidence feature priorities that can directly inform product roadmap. India now has limited primary data (27 vehicles, 10 interviews, Bangalore, EV cohort) that confirms navigation as the primary India purchase driver and reveals material preference differences from Europe (battery % over range; no music/call use during riding).
tensions.counterSignal: India's evidence base covers only 27 EV vehicles in a single city - not projectable to the broader ICE commuter market. Vietnam/SEA (41.7% of global connected motorcycle market) and Japan (HQ, strategic decision-making centre) still have zero direct rider voice. The India findings and European findings already diverge significantly, raising the risk that product decisions made from either alone will be wrong for the other.
tensions.theTension: Decisions made primarily from European evidence risk optimising for the wrong market. The limited India data now available shows the divergence is real and structural - navigation primacy, battery display preference, and near-zero music/call usage in India vs. theft alert priority, hazard navigation, and diagnostics in Europe. Acting without broader India and SEA rider research leaves the highest-growth market under-evidenced.

tensions.title: The Platform Tension: Shared Core vs. Regional Autonomy
tensions.signal: Building a single, shared global platform maximizes engineering efficiency, reduces costs, and allows for faster deployment of core updates. Regulatory requirements (GDPR, DPDP Act, PDPA, Vietnam data localisation) already create mandatory variation across regions.
tensions.counterSignal: Enabling deep regional differentiation (India-only security features, SEA battery-swap integration, European hazard alert data, Japan-specific navigation providers) adds significant complexity to the core platform and risks the fragmentation pattern documented in four-wheel automotive.
tensions.theTension: How to design a software platform that provides the economic benefits of a shared global core while enabling the necessary regional variation - without requiring a custom engineering project per market or drifting into an unmaintainable multi-codebase model.

tensions.title: The HQ Paradox Tension: Where Decisions Are Made vs. Where Growth Is
tensions.signal: Honda's strategic direction for the global 2W software programme originates from Japanese HQ, where the domestic market ranks #8 globally and connected services opportunity is rated Low. The markets that shape the strategic frame are not the markets driving connected vehicle growth.
tensions.counterSignal: India, Vietnam, and Indonesia - Honda's primary volume growth markets - are the segments where the connected motorcycle market is growing fastest (Asia-Pacific 35.8% CAGR). India now has limited first-party rider evidence (27 EV vehicles, 10 interviews), but Vietnam and Indonesia remain entirely undocumented, and the India sample is too narrow to substitute for broad India market research.
tensions.theTension: There is a structural risk that connected services strategy is calibrated to a Japan-domestic market context that is not representative of the markets where connected vehicle investment will generate the most return. Resolving this tension requires both market intelligence (Asia-Pacific rider research) and governance design (regional input into strategic prioritisation).

requirements.description: The ability to generate primary insight about rider needs, willingness to pay, and connected feature priorities in India, Vietnam, Indonesia, and Japan - not inferred from European primary research or from competitor feature presence. This requires a structured research capability equivalent to the European primary research (qualitative interviews + survey), operating in markets where language, income levels, commuter-vs-premium segment mix, and competitive landscape are substantially different from Europe.

A platform architecture enabling market-specific feature variation - India security features, SEA battery-swap integration, European hazard alert data, regional regulatory compliance layers (GDPR, DPDP Act, PDPA, Vietnam data localisation) - without forking the codebase or requiring a rebuild per market. The automotive fragmentation precedent suggests this needs to be a deliberate architectural decision made before regional stacks begin to diverge, not a retrofit after divergence has occurred.

The ability to connect to locally relevant partners (navigation providers such as Vietmap in Vietnam, payment rails, regulatory compliance systems, battery-swap networks) in each market without a custom engineering project per integration. The European finding that Google Maps and Waze dominate European rider navigation - combined with the Vietnam finding that Vietmap Live Pro is used for complex urban routing - illustrates that navigation data alone requires a configurable provider model rather than a single global integration.

A governance mechanism that brings regional market evidence (rider research, competitive dynamics, regulatory requirements) into product and technology strategy decisions in a structured way. Given that strategic direction originates from Japanese HQ while the primary growth markets are India and SEA, the governance design needs to account for the structural risk that decisions are made from a market context that is unrepresentative of where connected vehicle opportunity is largest.

title: Data as a Strategic Asset
domainConfidenceNote: Rider sentiment around data ownership is among the most consistently evidenced findings in the research - the "data held hostage" pattern appears across 20+ independent Western sources and is confirmed as the most anger-producing pattern in rider voice research. Competitor data platform investments by Ather, Hero, and TVS are confirmed from public technical disclosures, with Hero CVP providing a verified scale benchmark. European GDPR is confirmed as a regulatory constraint from the European rider voice research. Confidence is Medium rather than High because Honda's internal data ambitions and current collection capabilities rest on a single internal source (December 2024 executive statements) that has not been independently verified.

signals.label: Rider Data Hostage
signals.claim: Ride history becoming read-only on subscription cancellation is the most anger-producing pattern in rider voice research across forums and app reviews - riders frame it explicitly as data theft. The frustration is documented across third-party OEM app ecosystems: REVER ride history becomes read-only; Calimoto offline maps disappear on cancellation. Riders' stated position is "my rides, my data, permanently mine."
signals.sources: `wiki/meta/extractions_2026Q2_rider_voice_research.md` (Top Frustrations #3; Job 3 synthesis); MotoFlow blog; EatSleepRIDE platform; 20+ independent sources.

signals.label: Personalised Data-Back-to-Rider Model
signals.claim: Ather Energy's "Warped" annual personalised riding report is a documented model for returning data to riders as a valued experience rather than extracting it silently. The format - a personalised annual summary of riding behaviour, comparable to Spotify Wrapped - demonstrates that riders actively consent to data collection when it delivers direct, tangible value.
signals.sources: `wiki/meta/extractions_2026Q2_rider_voice_research.md` (Ather Energy engagement data cited as consent model).

signals.label: Competitor Platform Scale
signals.claim: Hero MotoCorp's Connected Vehicle Platform (CVP), built on AWS IoT Core and Excelfore eSync, handles 2.2 million API requests per day at 99.6% availability across 40,000 connected vehicles - a verified scale benchmark for vehicle data infrastructure. TVS Motor acquired telematics firm Intellicar and US AI firm Predictronics Corporation to build the PDX predictive maintenance platform and EV battery digital twins. CFMOTO's T-Box IoT gateway creates a real-time digital twin of each vehicle synced to cloud, enabling automated fault notifications and proactive maintenance alerts. Yamaha, Kawasaki, Suzuki, and Harley-Davidson show no evidence of equivalent dedicated data platform investment.
signals.sources: `wiki/meta/extractions_2026Q2_competitor_strategy_depth.md` (Hero: AWS case study, verified production metrics; TVS: Databricks Lakehouse, named CDO; CFMOTO: deep dive).

signals.label: European Regulatory Constraint
signals.claim: European GDPR is the primary data strategy constraint for any connected service deployed in Europe. Explicit consent is required before collecting personal ride data, and data portability on cancellation is expected by riders as a right rather than a feature. The European rider voice research (35 qualitative interviews, 301-rider EICMA survey) documents that European riders have a strong ownership orientation toward their vehicle and ride data.
signals.sources: `wiki/meta/extractions_2026Q2_europe_rider_voice_research.md` (Rider Voice, High confidence; methodology: DriveMode x Honda, Germany/France/Italy).

signals.label: Internal Ambition
signals.claim: Honda's connected services vision explicitly includes using data for defect detection, usage evaluation, and product development feedback loops.
signals.sources: `raw/2026Q2_organizational_capability_assessment.md`; single source - treat as directional intent, not confirmed capability.

signals.label: Data Collection Foundation
signals.claim: The RoadSync Duo app already collects location data, usage patterns, and user identifiers, creating a foundational data asset.
signals.sources: `sources/2026-06-23_honda_roadsync_duo_app_store.pdf`.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: Spotify Wrapped as the data-back-to-rider template
crossIndustryPatterns.body: Spotify's annual "Wrapped" personalised listening summary transformed passive data collection into a high-engagement, shareable moment that users actively anticipate. Ather Energy's "Warped" riding report applies the same model to motorcycle data. The pattern demonstrates that riders (and consumers broadly) consent to extensive data collection when the return is personal, tangible, and emotionally resonant - not when data flows only in one direction toward the platform.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: Data as a Service Enhancer (Modern)
crossIndustryPatterns.body: Leading technology companies primarily use customer data to improve their own core service - personalising recommendations, optimising logistics, refining product features. The data's value is realised by increasing customer retention and lifetime value, not by selling the data itself. Vehicle OEMs that have moved in this direction (Hero's predictive maintenance, TVS's battery digital twins) treat the data platform as a product quality asset rather than a monetisation channel.

crossIndustryPatterns.title: GDPR as competitive design constraint
crossIndustryPatterns.body: European financial services and health technology companies that invested early in privacy-by-design architecture - explicit consent flows, data portability APIs, right-to-erasure tooling - found that the compliance investment also reduced rider churn at cancellation by making data export a positive experience rather than a point of friction. Companies that treated GDPR as a compliance burden rather than a design constraint faced higher enforcement exposure and higher cancellation-related frustration.

implications: Data is not an asset to be extracted; it is a medium for a two-way relationship with the rider. The rider voice research across 20+ Western sources and the European primary research confirm that the single most anger-producing data practice is ride history becoming inaccessible on cancellation. The Ather "Warped" model and the broader Spotify Wrapped pattern suggest the higher-value position: returning data to riders in personalised, emotionally resonant forms converts passive data collection into active consent and loyalty. Hero CVP's scale (2.2M API requests/day, 99.6% availability) demonstrates that the infrastructure required to support this at fleet scale is not trivial - it requires purposeful investment in data engineering, not an extension of the app team's existing capacity. GDPR frames the European deployment as a design constraint that must be met from the outset: explicit consent architecture and data portability on cancellation are not optional features but baseline requirements. A strategy that treats data as an internal asset while ignoring portability and return-to-rider will face both regulatory exposure in Europe and trust erosion across all markets.

tensions.title: The Value Tension: Long-Term Platform Intelligence vs. Short-Term Privacy Risk
tensions.signal: Collecting rich, long-term rider and vehicle data is a prerequisite for the predictive maintenance, battery health, and product feedback loop capabilities that Hero, TVS, and CFMOTO are building as competitive infrastructure.
tensions.counterSignal: Every piece of data collected increases regulatory exposure under GDPR, cybersecurity risk, and the reputational damage potential of a breach or a perceived "data hostage" incident - the pattern already confirmed as the highest-anger trigger in rider voice research.
tensions.theTension: How to invest in the data infrastructure needed for long-term product intelligence while designing consent, portability, and return-to-rider experiences that prevent the data relationship from becoming the primary source of rider distrust.

tensions.title: The Control Tension: Centralised Data Platform vs. Rider Data Ownership
tensions.signal: To deliver predictive maintenance, personalised ride summaries, and fleet-level product intelligence, a centralised data platform with sufficient engineering depth is required - Hero CVP's 2.2M API requests/day benchmark indicates the scale of investment involved.
